---
title: "Research"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(knitr)
opts_chunk$set(fig.path = "images/",
               cache.path = "cache/",
               cache = FALSE,
               echo = FALSE, #set to false to hide code
               message = FALSE,
               warning = FALSE,
               out.width = "100%",
               dpi = 200,
               fig.align = "center")  
```

<script>
function hide_buttons(abstract) {
    var x = document.getElementById(abstract);
    if (x.style.display === "none") {
        x.style.display = "block";
    } else {
        x.style.display = "none";
    }
}
</script>

## Work in Progress

### Fairness, Pricing, and Modal Shift: Behavioral Perspectives on Urban Toll Acceptability

with [Philippe Gagnepain](https://www.parisschoolofeconomics.eu/personnes/philippe-gagnepain/) and [Vladimir Pecheu](https://www.parisschoolofeconomics.eu/personnes/carine-staropoli/) 

Reducing solo driving to lower greenhouse emissions and the associated negative externalities is essential. One potential solution is to increase the cost of car use through urban tolls, which have already been implemented in specific areas of cities like London and Stockholm. In France, these infrastructures are less common, one of the issues being the low public acceptability of these projects, even though the theoretical conditions of effectiveness are known. Thus, the necessity to carry out an experiment to test the predictions and investigate the behavioral responses to the implementation of a toll prevails. In this paper, we propose an original design to test the impact of tolls on modal shift and congestion in a specifically designed network. Participants were randomly categorized into two types of travelers, accounting for various access to public transportation. The preliminary results indicate that the implementation led to a decrease in car usage and that participants generally followed the Nash equilibrium predictions.  Additionally, acceptability was evaluated through voting and found to be greater when the toll costs were unequal between the two types in the network.


<br />

## Other Project

### Truth hurts: the dynamics of motivated mislearning  

with Lidia Esposito and [Luca Cocco](https://sites.google.com/view/lucacocco/overview?authuser=0)

<br />
